stories_covered: Databricks raises $5B at $134B valuation (32x revenue, 55% YoY growth)
Sierra hits $100M ARR at $10B valuation (100x multiple)
Eventbrite acquired for $500M (1.5x revenue with 50% premium)
Lovable hits $200M ARR; Databricks re-acceleration at scale
PagerDuty strategic stall despite distribution advantages
Salesforce Agent Force vs new AI-native startups
Workday sees seat-reduction as existential threat
harry_takes: OpenAI's 'code red' memo signals they're deprioritizing vertical expansion to focus on core product—a reversal of the everything-to-everyone strategy
The fundamental VC question is: how much premium do you pay for incremental growth? Databricks 32x vs Snowflake 20x shows market is willing to pay for acceleration
SaaS companies face a 'TAM trap'—most public SaaS grew into saturated markets with 7-10 competitors; there's no obvious second act
Efficiency per employee is rising everywhere; companies that don't improve ARR-per-headcount will see valuations compress
harry_boldest_quote: Salesforce could be the next Google.
predictions: Rory: Sierra will need to scale from $100M to $1B ARR to justify $10B valuation; enterprise change management is the rate limiter
Lemkin: Databricks and Snowflake will 'slug it out' for a decade like Oracle vs SAP; both survive but with compressed margins
Harry: Incumbent software (Salesforce, Workday) can win with Agent Force if they execute faster than startups, but organizational inertia makes this unlikely
Lemkin: 75% of existing 900 unicorns will never IPO due to finite TAM and incumbent competition
disagreements: Rory argued installed bases are assets because data and integrations provide moat; Lemkin countered that legacy customer support drains engineering capacity and prevents competing for new AI-native customers.
harry_role: Harry framed the central tension between growth and efficiency, introduced 'TAM trap' as diagnostic framework, and moderated the installed-base debate by asking whether data assets could be leveraged by incumbents or would leak value to AI agents sitting on top.
notable: The 'TAM Trap' concept emerged as the crystallizing insight: SaaS companies grew so thoroughly into their TAMs that the next generation of founders cannot find new markets; instead they must either expand TAM (via AI labor substitution) or steal from incumbents.
